nativeRange.habitat: Sandy and sandy-muddy substrates in open water and along the margins of Lake Malawi; found over sand flats at intermediate depths, often following sand-sifting cichlids and eartheaters to exploit disturbed substrates

decorPreferences: deep open sand substrate (fine sand, minimum 7–10 cm depth for sand-sifting)
minimal rockwork — open swimming lanes essential
smooth rocks or driftwood along tank margins for territory definition
avoid sharp-edged decor that may injure the nuchal hump
strong filtration to handle heavy bioload

diet.notes: Insectivore-leaning omnivore. In the wild feeds on invertebrates, small crustaceans, and organic matter sifted from sand — closely following other sand-sifting species (Taeniolethrinops, Aulonocara) to exploit disturbed substrate. In aquaria accepts high-quality cichlid pellets, Hikari Cichlid Gold, NLS pellets, frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, and krill. Supplement with occasional chopped earthworms or silversides. Avoid a heavy protein-only diet; include quality pellet base with spirulina component. Do not feed beef heart or other mammalian meats — can cause Malawi bloat.

goodWith: other large Lake Malawi Haplochromines of similar size
large Synodontis catfish (S. multipunctatus, S. petricola)
non-aggressive mbuna such as Labidochromis caeruleus in large tanks
avoidWith: aggressive mbuna (Melanochromis, Pseudotropheus crabro — excessive harassment)
very small fish and nano species (may be swallowed or harassed)
other hump-headed haps such as Placidochromis milomo (territory conflict)
South American or Central American cichlids (incompatible water chemistry and aggression mismatch)
invertebrates (shrimp and snails will be consumed)
conspecific males without sufficient tank length — male-male aggression intensifies at breeding

imageCandidates.notes: Adult male showing full nuchal hump and brilliant cobalt-blue coloration. FishBase species photo.
Adult male; good lateral profile showing hump. Confirm license on page before use.

summary: The Blue Dolphin Cichlid is a large, peaceful-for-its-size Lake Malawi Haplochromine instantly recognisable by the adult male’s pronounced forehead hump. Brilliant cobalt-blue adults make it one of the hobby’s most striking cichlids; juveniles are drab grey. Needs 400 L+ of hard alkaline water and a sandy substrate for natural sand-sifting behaviour.
careNotes:
Cyrtocara moorii is endemic to Lake Malawi and belongs to the Haplochromine tribe — the ‘Haps’ — alongside the peacock cichlids (Aulonocara). Unlike the notoriously aggressive mbuna, C. moorii is comparatively peaceful for its size, though males become territorial toward conspecifics and similar-shaped haps at breeding time. The minimum recommended tank is 400 L (approximately 150 cm footprint) to provide adequate territory and sand-sifting room; many experienced keepers recommend 500 L or more for a colony or mixed Hap-Peacock display.

Water chemistry must mirror Lake Malawi conditions: hard (gH 10–25), alkaline (pH 7.5–8.5), and well-buffered (kH 10–20). Temperature of 24–28°C is ideal. Frequent partial water changes of 25–30% weekly are essential given the high bioload of these large cichlids. A deep fine-sand substrate (minimum 7 cm) enables the natural sand-sifting foraging behaviour; the fish scoops mouthfuls of sand and sifts invertebrates through its gill rakers.

The most striking feature of mature males is the pronounced nuchal (forehead) hump, which gives the species its common names. The hump develops progressively from 12–15 cm onward and is more pronounced in dominant, well-fed males. Juveniles and females are drab grey with no hump; juveniles are sometimes sold as an unidentified ‘grey cichlid’ — the transformation to brilliant cobalt blue takes 18–24 months. Only one dominant male should be kept unless the tank is very large (600 L+).

In the wild, C. moorii is an opportunistic follower — it trails sand-sifting species such as Taeniolethrinops praeorbitalis and eartheaters, darting in to consume invertebrates and small organisms disturbed from the substrate. Replicating this is straightforward: keep alongside Aulonocara or other sand-disturbing haps. Malawi Bloat is the primary health risk; it is strongly associated with high-protein mammalian-meat diets (beef heart) and deteriorating water quality. Use quality cichlid pellets as the dietary staple and avoid over-feeding.
breedingNotes:
C. moorii is a maternal mouthbrooder — after spawning on the sand substrate, the female collects the eggs and incubates them in her buccal cavity for approximately 21–28 days. Females visibly ‘hold’ during this period, refusing food and displaying a distended jaw. Spawning in the aquarium is not uncommon in well-conditioned adults, but rearing fry to independence requires either a species tank or removal of the holding female to a dedicated brooding vessel to protect fry from predation.

The breeding colony setup typically comprises one male with two or three females; a ratio of 1:3 or more significantly reduces male harassment of spent females. Males may display aggressively toward other males and toward similar-coloured haps during courtship. Spawning typically occurs on flat sand or a smooth rock surface. Fry are released at approximately 10–12 mm and can accept micro-pellets, baby brine shrimp, and finely crushed flake from first feeding. A brood of 20–50 fry is typical. Breedability classification: moderate difficulty, primarily because of the tank-size requirement and the need to protect holding females.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Cyrtocara moorii
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size, temperature range (24–28°C), pH (7.5–8.5), hardness, native range (Lake Malawi), biotope, diet (invertivore/sand-sifter), compatibility notes, and breeding (maternal mouthbrooder, 21–28 day incubation). Tank size minimum 350–400 L with 150 cm footprint confirmed.

additional.name: FishBase — Cyrtocara moorii
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 25 cm TL males), temperature range, taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), native range (Lake Malawi — Malawi, Mozambique, Tanzania), depth range (3–15 m), and diet classification (invertivore). Confirmed monotypic genus.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Haplochromine Cichlid Care
additional.notes: General Hap care guidance used for tank size, water change regimen, and diet recommendations (cichlid pellet-based diet, avoidance of mammalian protein/Malawi Bloat risk). Species not individually listed but Hap-Peacock compatibility matrix applies directly.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Cyrtocara moorii profile
additional.notes: Supplementary source. Confirmed nuchal hump development timeline, juvenile coloration (drab grey), adult male brilliant blue, recommended group ratio (1 male : 2–3 females), and minimum tank size guidance. Also confirmed sand-follower foraging ecology.

additional.name: Cichlid-Forum — Cyrtocara moorii species profile
additional.notes: Community expert profile corroborating mouthbrooder incubation period (21–28 days), fry size at release (~10–12 mm), brood size (20–50 fry), and sand-sifting follower behaviour with Aulonocara and Taeniolethrinops in the wild. Also noted conspecific male aggression and hump prominence as dominance indicator.

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder; female incubates eggs buccally for 21–28 days and releases free-swimming fry at ~10–12 mm. Spawning is achievable in home aquaria with 1:2–3 male-to-female ratio. Fry vulnerable to predation; holding female ideally moved to a separate brooding tank. Brood size 20–50.
